title: Merge k Sorted Lists
description:
You're handed `lists`, a collection of `k` singly linked lists, each one already arranged in non-decreasing order and represented as a plain list of its values. Weave all `k` of them together into one combined list that stays sorted the whole way through, and return its values from head to tail.

**Example**

```
lists = [[1, 4, 5], [1, 3, 4], [2, 6]]
merge_k_lists(lists)  ->  [1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5, 6]
```
starter_code:
def merge_k_lists(lists):
    # lists: list of k sorted lists of values, each representing a linked list.
    # Return the fully merged, sorted list of values.
    pass
hints: Build each of the k lists as real nodes, then push the head of every non-empty list into a min-heap keyed by value (with a tiebreaker index to keep tuples comparable when values are equal).
Repeatedly pop the smallest node from the heap, append its value to the output, and if that node has a next, push the next node into the heap — this is the k-way merge, O(n log k).

solutions.name: Min-heap k-way merge
solutions.explanation: Build each input list into real linked-list nodes. Push a (value, tiebreak_index, node) tuple for the head of every non-empty list onto a min-heap; the tiebreak index prevents comparing node objects directly when values tie. Repeatedly pop the smallest tuple, append its value to the result, and if that node has a next node, push it onto the heap (reusing a fresh unique tiebreak index). This produces the fully sorted output in O(n log k) time, where n is the total number of values and k is the number of lists, since the heap never holds more than k elements.
solutions.complexity: Time O(n log k), Space O(k) for the heap (plus O(n) to build/return the values)
